Interview keyword: Triage, investigate, fix+prevent — same pattern, different tool per issue.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you use the ABAP debugger to inspect internal table content?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: ABAP debugger mein, line of interest pe breakpoint set karo. Jab execution stop ho, variable monitor mein internal table variable name enter karo ya code mein double-click karo. Table content viewer saari rows ko saare fields ke saath dikhata hai. Aap rows navigate kar sakte ho, specific values search kar sakte ho, aur field-level content check kar sakte ho. ASSIGNING scenarios ke liye, currently process ho rahi row dekhne ke liye FIELD-SYMBOL inspect karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: In the ABAP debugger, set a breakpoint at the line of interest. When execution stops, enter the internal table variable name in the variable monitor or double-click it in the code. The table content viewer shows all rows with all fields. You can navigate rows, search for specific values, and check field-level content. For ASSIGNING scenarios, inspect the FIELD-SYMBOL to see the current row being processed.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Debugging tip: use 'watchpoint' on an internal table variable — debugger stops whenever the table content changes. Useful for tracking unexpected modifications to an internal table from multiple code locations.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Setting too many unconditional breakpoints and stepping through thousands of loop iterations — use conditional breakpoints (specific key value) to stop only on the relevant row.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Conditional Breakpoint Setup Guide:
" 1. Set breakpoint inside LOOP
" 2. Right-click breakpoint → Condition
" 3. Enter condition: ls_item-matnr = 'SUSPECT_MAT'
" 4. Debugger only stops when that material is processed
" Saves time vs stepping through 10000 rows

" Watchpoint for unexpected table changes:
" Debugger → Watchpoints → Add Variable
" Variable: lt_orders (table name)
" Type: Change watchpoint
" Debugger stops whenever lt_orders is modified

followupAnswerBank.question: What is a conditional breakpoint and when do you use it for internal table debugging?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Conditional breakpoint sirf tab trigger hota hai jab specified condition true ho. Large tables ke saath internal table debugging ke liye, LOOP ke andar breakpoint set karo us specific key value pe condition ke saath jise aap investigate kar rahe ho — e.g., ls_item-vbeln = '4500001234'. Ye hazaron unrelated rows ke through stepping avoid karta hai aur directly issue cause kar rahi row pe stop karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: A conditional breakpoint triggers only when a specified condition is true. For internal table debugging with large tables, set a breakpoint inside the LOOP with condition on the specific key value you are investigating — e.g., ls_item-vbeln = '4500001234'. This avoids stepping through thousands of unrelated rows and directly stops at the row causing the issue.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Large volume debugging: 80,000 purchase orders in loop, one specific PO was getting wrong price. Conditional breakpoint on that PO number — debugger stopped exactly there, condition clearly visible. Fix found in 10 minutes instead of hours.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using unconditional breakpoints in large loops and hitting F8 repeatedly — too slow, wastes debugging session time, often gives up and tries another approach.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Loop with 80,000 rows - use conditional breakpoint:
LOOP AT lt_po_items INTO DATA(ls_item).
  " Set breakpoint on this line with condition:
  " ls_item-ebeln = '4500099999'  (the problem PO)
  DATA(lv_price) = calculate_price( ls_item ).
  ls_item-netwr = lv_price.
  MODIFY lt_po_items FROM ls_item.
ENDLOOP.
" Debugger stops only for that specific PO
" Inspect ls_item and lv_price for that row only

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you debug a FOR ALL ENTRIES issue where unexpected rows are returned?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Unexpected FAE results debug karo pehle driving table content inspect karke — kya duplicates hain? Unexpected entries? Phir FAE SELECT ke baad result table inspect karo — kya extra rows driving table mein duplicates se aa rahi hain jo separate WHERE conditions ke roop mein bheji ja rahi hain? FAE WHERE clause mein used saare fields pe driving table ko deduplicate karta hai — agar aapki driving table mein same VBELN different MATNR ke saath hai, dono rows separate conditions ke roop mein bheji jaati hain, potentially expected se zyada fetch karte hue.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Debug unexpected FAE results by first inspecting the driving table content — are there duplicates? Unexpected entries? Then inspect the result table after FAE SELECT — are extra rows coming from duplicates in the driving table being sent as separate WHERE conditions? FAE deduplicates the driving table on all fields used in the WHERE clause — if your driving table has the same VBELN with different MATNR, both rows are sent as separate conditions, potentially fetching more than expected.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Debugging FAE extra rows: driving table had 500 entries but 700 result rows came back. Root cause: driving table had duplicate VBELNs with different item fields — FAE sent both as conditions, fetching items for duplicated order numbers twice. Fix: SORT + DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES on driving table on the JOIN key field before FAE.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Assuming FAE always returns exactly one result row per driving table row — FAE is a JOIN equivalent, result count depends on matching rows in target table.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" FAE extra rows debug:
DATA lt_headers TYPE TABLE OF vbak.
DATA lt_items   TYPE TABLE OF vbap.
" Check driving table BEFORE FAE:
" Breakpoint here - inspect lt_headers
" Are there duplicates on VBELN?
SORT lt_headers BY vbeln.
DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES FROM lt_headers COMPARING vbeln.
" NOW run FAE with clean driving table:
IF lt_headers IS NOT INITIAL.
  SELECT vbeln, posnr, matnr, kwmeng
    INTO TABLE @lt_items
    FROM vbap
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @lt_headers
    WHERE vbeln = @lt_headers-vbeln.
ENDIF.
" Breakpoint after - inspect lt_items count

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you debug an internal table SORT issue where output order seems wrong?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SORT order issues debug karo SORT ke baad breakpoint set karke aur debugger mein first 5-10 rows inspect karke. Verify karo ki sort key fields correct hain — multi-field sorts order mein apply hote hain (first field primary, second secondary). Type issues check karo: numeric data wala character field lexicographically sort hota hai, numerically nahi. Per field ASCENDING vs DESCENDING verify karo. Character fields pe case sensitivity order affect karti hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Debug SORT order issues by setting a breakpoint after SORT and inspecting the first 5-10 rows in the debugger. Verify the sort key fields are correct — multi-field sorts apply in order (first field primary, second secondary). Check for type issues: a character field containing numeric data sorts lexicographically not numerically. Verify ASCENDING vs DESCENDING per field. Case sensitivity on character fields affects order.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Wrong sort order ticket: report sorted by delivery date but '20241210' was appearing before '20241201' at times. Root cause: erdat field was TYPE C LENGTH 8 instead of TYPE D — lexicographic sort of character dates is usually correct but was breaking due to a legacy data entry issue with '0' prefixes. Fix: convert to TYPE D or use CAST in SELECT.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Assuming SORT on a 'date' character field works like date sort — TYPE C fields sort lexicographically. Always use TYPE D for date fields to ensure correct chronological sorting.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Debugging sort order issues:
SORT lt_orders BY erdat DESCENDING vbeln.
" <<< BREAKPOINT after SORT
" Inspect first 5 rows:
" Row 1: erdat should be latest date
" Row 2: same or older date
" If wrong: check erdat TYPE in structure

" Fix for character date field:
SELECT vbeln, erdat
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_sorted)
  FROM vbak ORDER BY erdat DESCENDING.
" Or ensure TYPE D in structure:
TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_order,
         vbeln TYPE vbak-vbeln,
         erdat TYPE d,  " TYPE D ensures correct date sort
       END OF ty_order.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you debug a COLLECT that is producing wrong totals?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: COLLECT wrong totals debug karo loop ke andar har COLLECT call se pehle breakpoint set karke. Collected ho rahe work area ko inspect karo: verify karo ki key field values correct hain (wrong key = wrong aggregation group) aur numeric field values pre-COLLECT correct hain. Loop ke baad, result table inspect karo: verify karo ki row count unique keys ke equal hai aur totals data ke subset ke liye manual calculation se match karte hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Debug COLLECT wrong totals by setting a breakpoint inside the loop before each COLLECT call. Inspect the work area being collected: verify key field values are correct (wrong key = wrong aggregation group) and numeric field values are correct pre-COLLECT. After loop, inspect result table: verify row count equals unique keys and totals match manual calculation for a subset of data.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: COLLECT wrong total ticket: material totals were 10x higher than expected. Debugger showed the numeric field menge was of type QUAN with implicit decimal handling — the actual stored value was 1000000 but displayed as 1000.000. COLLECT summed the raw stored values correctly but display formatting was missing decimal conversion. Bug was in display layer, not COLLECT.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Blaming COLLECT when the issue is actually in how the numeric field is defined or displayed — COLLECT correctly aggregates what it receives. Verify the input values are what you expect.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" COLLECT wrong total debug:
DATA lt_result TYPE TABLE OF ty_mat_qty.
DATA ls_collect TYPE ty_mat_qty.

LOOP AT lt_movements INTO DATA(ls_mov).
  ls_collect-matnr     = ls_mov-matnr.     " key field
  ls_collect-total_qty = ls_mov-menge.     " numeric field
  " <<< BREAKPOINT: inspect ls_collect before COLLECT
  " Verify: ls_collect-matnr = expected key?
  " Verify: ls_collect-total_qty = expected qty value?
  COLLECT ls_collect INTO lt_result.
ENDLOOP.
" <<< BREAKPOINT: inspect lt_result
" Verify: lines( lt_result ) = unique material count
" Verify: lt_result[ matnr = 'M001' ]-total_qty = expected sum

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you use system variables to debug internal table operations?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Internal table debugging ke liye key system variables: sy-subrc (0=success, 4=not found) — har READ TABLE aur DELETE ke baad check karo; sy-tabix (current LOOP index ya last READ TABLE index) — correct position pe MODIFY ke liye useful; sy-tfill (partial key ke saath READ TABLE ke baad table mein rows). Debugger mein, READ TABLE ke turant baad sy-subrc hamesha inspect karo — forgotten sy-subrc check silent wrong-data bugs ka ek common source hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Key system variables for internal table debugging: sy-subrc (0=success, 4=not found) — check after every READ TABLE and DELETE; sy-tabix (current LOOP index or last READ TABLE index) — useful for MODIFY at correct position; sy-tfill (rows in table after READ TABLE with partial key). In the debugger, always inspect sy-subrc immediately after READ TABLE — a forgotten sy-subrc check is a common source of silent wrong-data bugs.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Debugging tip: add a watch on sy-subrc in the debugger variable panel. As you step through READ TABLE calls, sy-subrc updates automatically. Immediately shows whether each lookup succeeded or failed without needing to inspect the work area.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Not checking sy-subrc after READ TABLE and processing the work area anyway — if sy-subrc = 4 the work area has stale data from a previous successful READ. Always check sy-subrc.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" System variables in internal table debugging:
READ TABLE lt_config INTO DATA(ls_cfg)
  WITH KEY param = 'THRESHOLD' BINARY SEARCH.
" <<< BREAKPOINT: inspect sy-subrc immediately
" sy-subrc = 0: key found, ls_cfg has valid data
" sy-subrc = 4: key not found, ls_cfg has old data!
" sy-tabix: row index where found (use for MODIFY)
IF sy-subrc = 0.
  MODIFY lt_config FROM ls_cfg INDEX sy-tabix.
ELSE.
  " Handle not-found explicitly
  MESSAGE |Config THRESHOLD not found| TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.
" After LOOP:
LOOP AT lt_orders INTO DATA(ls_o).
" sy-tabix = current row index (1, 2, 3...)
ENDLOOP.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you debug memory-related internal table crashes in production without a debugger?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Bina debugger ke production memory crashes ke liye: ST22 crash moment pe variable values ke saath short dump deta hai — wahan dikhayi gayi internal table sizes inspect karo. SM21 crash time ke around system alerts dikhata hai. Code mein proactive memory management add karo: use ke turant baad large internal tables FREE karo, unfiltered large tables kabhi load mat karo. Ongoing monitoring ke liye, next crash se pehle memory trends watch karne ke liye SM50 ya ABAP Memory Inspector (agar available hai) use karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: For production memory crashes without a debugger: ST22 gives the short dump with variable values at the crash moment — inspect the internal table sizes shown there. SM21 shows system alerts around the crash time. Add proactive memory management in code: FREE large internal tables immediately after use, never load unfiltered large tables. For ongoing monitoring, use SM50 or the ABAP Memory Inspector (if available) to watch memory trends before the next crash.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Production crash investigation without debugger: ST22 showed MEMORY_NO_MORE_PAGING dump. Dump details showed internal table lt_all_invoices with 2.3M rows at time of crash. Code inspection confirmed SELECT FROM rbkp without WHERE clause — full table load. Fix: mandatory input parameter for company code and year, reducing scope from 2.3M rows to ~12k rows. No more crash.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Enabling the ABAP debugger in production to investigate a crash — this can interfere with live users and cause additional issues. Always prefer post-mortem analysis via ST22/SM21.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Proactive memory management for large tables:
DATA lt_large TYPE TABLE OF rbkp.

" Bounded SELECT - never full table
SELECT * INTO TABLE @lt_large
  FROM rbkp
  WHERE bukrs = @lv_bukrs
    AND gjahr = @lv_gjahr.  " mandatory filters

" Process the data
LOOP AT lt_large ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<inv>).
  " processing...
ENDLOOP.

" Free memory immediately after use
FREE lt_large.  " releases memory, table becomes empty
" Continue with other logic
" Total memory footprint stays bounded

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between debugging a standard table vs a hashed table issue?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Standard table debugging: iteration order insertion order hai, READ TABLE BINARY SEARCH ke saath pehle SORT chahiye, READ ke baad MODIFY ke liye sy-tabix reliable. Hashed table debugging: LOOP mein iteration order undefined hai — sequence pe rely mat karo; ek typed hashed/sorted table pe APPEND khud ek SYNTAX error hai (compile time); READ TABLE ko WITH TABLE KEY use karna padta hai — hashed/sorted table pe BINARY SEARCH use karna bhi syntax check pe pakda jaata hai, runtime pe nahi. Debugger mein, hashed table display order aapko surprise kar sakta hai — undefined order ko wrong data ke saath confuse mat karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Standard table debugging: iteration order is insertion order, READ TABLE with BINARY SEARCH needs SORT first, sy-tabix reliable for MODIFY after READ. Hashed table debugging: iteration order in LOOP is undefined — do not rely on sequence; APPEND on a typed hashed/sorted table is itself a SYNTAX error (compile time); READ TABLE must use WITH TABLE KEY — using BINARY SEARCH on a hashed/sorted table is also caught at the syntax check, not at runtime. In the debugger, hashed table display order may surprise you — do not confuse undefined order with wrong data.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Modern ABAP rejects BINARY SEARCH on hashed/sorted tables at the SYNTAX CHECK (compile time), not runtime. Debugging hashed table issue: developer wrote READ TABLE lt_hashed WITH KEY field = val BINARY SEARCH — the program failed to activate with a syntax error from SLIN/ATC. Fix: change to WITH TABLE KEY field = val. Simple one-word change but required understanding that the check happens at compile time, not at runtime.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using BINARY SEARCH keyword with hashed/sorted tables — caught at SYNTAX CHECK (compile time), not at runtime. Hashed tables do not need BINARY SEARCH; they use a hash function automatically. Also: APPEND on a typed hashed/sorted table is itself a syntax error.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Standard Table Debug:
DATA lt_std TYPE TABLE OF ty_order.
SORT lt_std BY vbeln.  " needed for BINARY SEARCH
READ TABLE lt_std WITH KEY vbeln = '001' BINARY SEARCH INTO DATA(ls).
" sy-tabix = row index found, reliable for MODIFY

" Hashed Table Debug:
DATA lt_hash TYPE HASHED TABLE OF ty_order
  WITH UNIQUE KEY vbeln.
" WRONG - syntax error at compile time (not runtime):
" READ TABLE lt_hash WITH KEY vbeln = '001' BINARY SEARCH.
" CORRECT:
READ TABLE lt_hash WITH TABLE KEY vbeln = '001' INTO DATA(ls_h).
" No SORT needed, no BINARY SEARCH keyword
" Iteration order in LOOP is undefined - do not depend on it

internal-tables-037.easyUnderstanding:
Internal tables mein performance problems bohot common hain SAP mein — aur yeh problems sirf bade tables pe hi nahi, chhote tables pe bhi ho sakte hain galat approach se.

Sabse common performance problem: SELECT inside LOOP. Har loop iteration pe ek database call — 10,000 rows mein 10,000 database calls. Yeh N+1 problem hai aur performance kill karta hai.

Doosra: FOR ALL ENTRIES bina empty check ke. Agar driving table empty hai toh full database table load ho jaati hai. Lakhon rows memory mein — system crash.

Teesra: Standard table pe READ TABLE bina BINARY SEARCH — har read O(n) hai. 50,000 rows mein 25,000 average comparisons per lookup. Table size badhe toh time linearly badhe.

Chauthaa: SELECT * — saare columns laana jab sirf 3-4 chahiye. Network traffic aur memory dono waste.

Paanchva: Redundant SORT — ek hi table ko baar baar sort karna LOOP se pehle, bina zaroorat ke.

Chehva: Deep copy in LOOP INTO — har iteration pe structure copy. Large structure aur large table — combined overhead significant hota hai.

Saathva: Memory overflow — bahut badi internal table bina bounds ke — program ya system crash.

In sab ko avoid karo — performance automatically bahut improve ho jaati hai. SAT transaction se measure karo before and after.
internal-tables-037.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Internal table performance problems ka technical deep-dive karte hain — har problem ka O-notation, real impact, aur fix.

1. SELECT inside LOOP (N+1 Problem):
Complexity: O(n) DB calls. 10,000 rows = 10,000 roundtrips to DB. Each DB call ~0.5-2ms on LAN — 10,000 × 1ms = 10 seconds just in DB round-trip overhead. Plus actual query execution per call. Fix: single SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES before loop = O(1) DB call. Improvement: 100x to 1000x on real data.

2. Unfiltered FAE (Empty Driving Table):
Complexity: O(all rows in target table). VBAP table with 10M rows — full scan. Fix: IS NOT INITIAL check = O(0) if empty, normal FAE if not. Non-negotiable.

3. Standard Table READ Without BINARY SEARCH:
Complexity: O(n) per read, O(n²) total for n reads. 50,000 rows, 50,000 reads = 2.5 billion comparisons worst case. BINARY SEARCH: O(log n) per read = 50,000 × 16 = 800,000 comparisons. Hashed table: O(1) per read = 50,000 × 1 = 50,000 operations. Choose wisely.

4. SELECT * Without Field List:
Network and memory waste. MARA has 200+ columns — fetching all when only MATNR, MAKTX needed means 50x more data. Use SELECT field1 field2 always.

5. Redundant SORT Operations:
SORT complexity: O(n log n). If LOOP karo → partial SORT → LOOP again → SORT again — repeated sorts waste CPU. Solution: sort once before all reads, use SORTED table type (maintained automatically), or pre-sort via SELECT ORDER BY.

6. LOOP INTO Wide Structure (Deep Copy Overhead):
Each LOOP INTO copies entire row. 100-field structure × 100,000 rows = 10 million field copies. LOOP ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL: zero copies, pointer access. On large loops with wide structures, ASSIGNING is measurably faster.

7. Memory Overflow:
No WHERE clause on large table → all rows in memory. VBAK with 20M rows × average row size 200 bytes = 4GB internal table. Beyond work process memory limit → MEMORY_NO_MORE_PAGING dump. Fix: always bounded SELECT with WHERE clause, mandatory input parameters.

8. Nested LOOP (Cartesian Join in ABAP):
LOOP AT lt_a. LOOP AT lt_b WHERE key = ls_a-key. — if lt_a has 1000 rows and lt_b has 1000 rows with no filtering, worst case 1M iterations. Use READ TABLE instead of inner LOOP for single-row lookup.

9. APPEND in LOOP Without Initial Estimate:
Repeated APPEND to standard table causes internal memory reallocation at certain thresholds. For large tables, use INITIAL SIZE hint in table declaration or pre-allocate.
internal-tables-037.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'The most common performance problems I have seen with internal tables are: SELECT inside LOOP causing N+1 database calls — this is the top performance killer in ABAP; unfiltered large table loads without WHERE clauses; and READ TABLE on standard tables without BINARY SEARCH causing O(n) sequential scans.'

'I address these by: pre-fetching lookup data before loops using SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES; always using SORTED or HASHED table types for high-frequency READ TABLE operations; and restricting SELECT to only needed fields and rows.'

'I measure performance using SAT transaction — it shows exactly how many database calls a program makes and which statements consume the most time. For memory, ST12 and SM50 show the footprint. I validate fixes by comparing SAT traces before and after the change.'

Hinglish tip: Performance question pe always N+1 problem mention karo pehle — yeh sabse common aur high-impact hai. Phir table type selection mention karo — SORTED vs HASHED. Aur SAT mention karo — measurement tool jaanna professional hai.
internal-tables-037.codeExamples:
" === Problem 1: N+1 SELECT in LOOP ===
" BAD (N DB calls):
LOOP AT lt_orders INTO DATA(ls_o).
  SELECT SINGLE maktx FROM makt INTO @DATA(lv_desc)
    WHERE matnr = @ls_o-matnr AND spras = 'EN'.
ENDLOOP.

" GOOD (1 DB call):
IF lt_orders IS NOT INITIAL.
  SELECT matnr, maktx
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_makt)
    FROM makt
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @lt_orders
    WHERE matnr = @lt_orders-matnr AND spras = 'EN'.
ENDIF.
SORT lt_makt BY matnr.
LOOP AT lt_orders ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<o>).
  READ TABLE lt_makt WITH KEY matnr = <o>-matnr
    INTO DATA(ls_desc) BINARY SEARCH.
  IF sy-subrc = 0. <o>-maktx = ls_desc-maktx. ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.

" === Problem 3: Sequential Scan vs Binary Search ===
" BAD - O(n) per read:
DATA lt_std TYPE TABLE OF ty_mat.
READ TABLE lt_std WITH KEY matnr = 'M001' INTO DATA(ls).

" GOOD - O(1) always:
DATA lt_hash TYPE HASHED TABLE OF ty_mat
  WITH UNIQUE KEY matnr.
READ TABLE lt_hash WITH TABLE KEY matnr = 'M001' INTO DATA(ls2).
internal-tables-037.interviewScriptHinglish: sabse common internal table performance problems hain: SELECT inside LOOP jo N+1 database calls banata hai — ABAP mein top killer; unfiltered SELECT * large tables pe jo memory flood karta hai; aur READ TABLE on standard tables bina BINARY SEARCH ke jo O(n) sequential scans banata hai. Fixes hain: SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES se lookup data pre-fetch karna IS NOT INITIAL se guarded, high-frequency reads ke liye SORTED ya HASHED table types use karna, aur sirf needed fields aur rows SELECT karna. SAT execution counts aur runtime measure karta hai, ST12 aur SM50 memory consumption expose karte hain, aur before/after traces validate karte hain ki har fix real hai na ki anecdotal.
internal-tables-037.interviewScriptEnglish: The most common internal table performance problems are: SELECT inside LOOP causing N+1 database calls — the top killer in ABAP; unfiltered SELECT * on large tables that flood memory; and READ TABLE on standard tables without BINARY SEARCH causing O(n) sequential scans. The fixes are: pre-fetch lookup data with SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES guarded by IS NOT INITIAL, use SORTED or HASHED table types for high-frequency reads, and SELECT only the needed fields and rows. SAT measures execution counts and runtime, ST12 and SM50 expose memory consumption, and before/after traces validate that each fix is real and not anecdotal.

wordByWordSamjho.keyword: SELECT SINGLE maktx FROM makt ... INSIDE LOOP
wordByWordSamjho.meaning: Anti-pattern producing one database call per outer row.
Bulk fetch replacing the N+1 pattern with batched queries.
Mandatory guard preventing full table scans on empty driver.
Prerequisite for BINARY SEARCH on the standard description table.

wordByWordSamjho.keyword: READ TABLE ... WITH KEY matnr = ... BINARY SEARCH
wordByWordSamjho.meaning: O(log n) lookup used in the main LOOP after pre-fetch.

wordByWordSamjho.keyword: HASHED TABLE WITH UNIQUE KEY matnr
wordByWordSamjho.meaning: O(1) lookup table eliminating linear scans on master data.

wordByWordSamjho.keyword: READ TABLE lt_hash WITH TABLE KEY matnr = ...
wordByWordSamjho.meaning: Optimised hash access on the cached lookup table.
Profiling and memory monitoring transactions used to validate fixes.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Calling SELECT SINGLE inside an outer LOOP to enrich rows.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Generates N database round trips, dominating runtime and showing up as the top finding in ST05 and SAT.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Pre-fetch with SELECT ... FOR ALL ENTRIES guarded by IS NOT INITIAL and use READ TABLE WITH KEY BINARY SEARCH or hashed access inside the loop.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Loading large tables with SELECT * and no WHERE clause.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Pulls hundreds of columns and millions of rows, exhausting memory and triggering TSV dumps.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Use specific field lists, mandatory WHERE conditions and PACKAGE SIZE chunking when dealing with very large datasets.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Reading standard tables WITH KEY without BINARY SEARCH or hash conversion.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Linear O(n) per read multiplied by outer loop iterations yields quadratic runtime on big data.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Convert to HASHED TABLE WITH UNIQUE KEY for exact matches, or SORT + BINARY SEARCH for sorted standard tables.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Repeatedly sorting the same internal table inside a loop.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: SORT is O(n log n); doing it per iteration multiplies the cost dramatically.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Sort once outside the loop and either keep a sorted table type or rely on sy-tabix and parallel cursor patterns inside.

internal-tables-037.quickRevisionNotes:
What: Catalogue of common internal table performance problems and their fixes.

Used for:
- Code reviews and ATC remediation
- Performance audits of legacy reports and batch jobs
- Convincing stakeholders with measured before/after numbers

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- IF lt IS NOT INITIAL + FOR ALL ENTRIES; SORT once + BINARY SEARCH
- HASHED TABLE WITH UNIQUE KEY; SELECT specific fields with WHERE
- Profile and validate with SAT, ST05, ST12, SM50

Interview keyword: N+1, unfiltered loads, sequential reads — three patterns, three fixes.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the O(n²) problem in internal tables and how do you fix it?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: O(n²) tab hota hai jab ek LOOP ke andar related rows ke liye doosra LOOP search karta hai — classic nested LOOP without indexing. 1,000 orders har ek 1,000 items tak check karne ke liye: 1 million iterations. Fix: inner LOOP ko sorted table pe READ TABLE BINARY SEARCH se replace karo, ya total O(n) ke liye HASHED table use karo. Better: separate tables ke bajaye pre-joined data paane ke liye database JOIN use karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: O(n²) occurs when a LOOP contains another LOOP searching for related rows — classic nested LOOP without indexing. For 1,000 orders each with up to 1,000 items checked: 1 million iterations. Fix: replace the inner LOOP with READ TABLE BINARY SEARCH on a sorted table, or use a HASHED table for O(n) total. Better: use a database JOIN to get pre-joined data instead of separate tables.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Found O(n²) in a production report: outer LOOP on 5,000 deliveries, inner LOOP on 50,000 delivery items. Report runtime: 45 minutes. Replaced inner LOOP with READ TABLE on SORTED delivery items table. Runtime: 12 seconds. Exactly O(n²) → O(n log n) improvement.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Writing nested LOOPs without realizing the quadratic complexity — acceptable with small data in development, catastrophic in production with real data volumes.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" O(n²) - nested LOOP (BAD):
LOOP AT lt_deliveries INTO DATA(ls_del).
  LOOP AT lt_del_items INTO DATA(ls_item)   " O(n²)!
    WHERE vbeln = ls_del-vbeln.
    " process item...
  ENDLOOP.
ENDLOOP.
" Fix: SORTED table + READ TABLE (O(n log n)):
SORT lt_del_items BY vbeln.
LOOP AT lt_deliveries INTO DATA(ls_del2).
  READ TABLE lt_del_items WITH KEY vbeln = ls_del2-vbeln
    BINARY SEARCH INTO DATA(ls_first_item).
  CHECK sy-subrc = 0.
  " Process items from sy-tabix onwards
ENDLOOP.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does SELECT * impact internal table performance?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SELECT * ek table ke saare columns fetch karta hai — MARA (200+ columns) ya BKPF (100+ columns) jaisi wide tables ke liye, iska matlab hai network pe massive amounts unnecessary data transfer karna aur memory mein store karna. Hamesha sirf needed fields specify karo. Ye network I/O, internal table memory footprint, aur har LOOP iteration pe wide structure copy karne ka ABAP processing time reduce karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: SELECT * fetches all columns of a table — for wide tables like MARA (200+ columns) or BKPF (100+ columns), this means transferring massive amounts of unnecessary data over the network and storing it in memory. Always specify only needed fields. This reduces network I/O, internal table memory footprint, and ABAP processing time for copying the wide structure on each LOOP iteration.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Optimization: replaced SELECT * FROM bkpf with SELECT belnr bukrs bldat dmbtr FROM bkpf. Table has 120 columns, only 4 needed. Internal table memory dropped from 2.4GB to 80MB for the same 20M rows volume in terms of structure size. Same data, 30x less memory.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using SELECT * for convenience during development and forgetting to restrict fields before production — common shortcut that becomes a production performance issue.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" BAD: fetches all 200+ columns from MARA:
SELECT * INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_mara_all) FROM mara
  WHERE matnr IN @lr_matnr.
" Internal table: 200 fields × rows = huge memory

" GOOD: only 3 needed fields:
SELECT matnr, mtart, matkl
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_mara_slim)
  FROM mara
  WHERE matnr IN @lr_matnr.
" Internal table: 3 fields × rows = 67x less memory
" Network transfer: 67x less data
" LOOP processing: 67x less copy overhead per row

followupAnswerBank.question: When does HANA pushdown help with internal table performance?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: HANA pushdown ka matlab hai HANA ko computation database layer pe execute karne dena rows ko ABAP mein fetch karke wahan process karne ke bajaye. Aggregation (SUM, COUNT, MAX) ke liye, SELECT mein GROUP BY work ko HANA ke column store pe push karta hai — sirf aggregated result ABAP mein aata hai. Filtering ke liye, SELECT mein WHERE clause saari rows load karke LOOP mein filter karne se zyada efficient hai. HANA-native CDS views aur optimize karte hain HANA-specific execution plans ke saath.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: HANA pushdown means letting HANA execute computation at the database layer rather than fetching rows into ABAP and processing there. For aggregation (SUM, COUNT, MAX), GROUP BY in SELECT pushes the work to HANA's column store — only the aggregated result arrives in ABAP. For filtering, a WHERE clause in SELECT is more efficient than loading all rows and filtering in a LOOP. HANA-native CDS views further optimize with HANA-specific execution plans.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Replaced ABAP COLLECT-loop aggregation of 8M movement rows (load all into internal table, COLLECT to sum) with SELECT SUM GROUP BY from HANA. Runtime: 12 minutes → 45 seconds. Same result, computation moved to HANA column store where it belongs.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Loading all rows into ABAP internal table for aggregation when a SELECT GROUP BY pushes it to HANA — classic missed optimization in S/4HANA projects.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" ABAP-side aggregation (SLOW for large data):
SELECT * INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_all_moves)  " 8M rows!
  FROM mseg WHERE mjahr = '2024'.
DATA lt_totals TYPE TABLE OF ty_mat_qty.
LOOP AT lt_all_moves INTO DATA(ls). COLLECT ls INTO lt_totals. ENDLOOP.

" HANA pushdown (FAST - 1000 rows returned, not 8M):
SELECT matnr, SUM( menge ) AS total_qty
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_hana_totals)
  FROM mseg
  WHERE mjahr = '2024'
  GROUP BY matnr.
" HANA computes SUM on 8M rows, returns 1000 aggregated rows
" 8000x less data in ABAP memory

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle performance issues caused by redundant internal table sorts?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Redundant SORT CPU waste karta hai — 50,000 rows pe har SORT O(n log n) operations cost karta hai. Common anti-pattern: LOOP ke andar SORT ya same table pe multiple SORT calls. Fix: saari READ TABLE calls se pehle ek baar sort karo; ya SORTED table type use karo jo har APPEND pe order automatically maintain karta hai; ya SELECT ... ORDER BY use karo database se pre-sorted data receive karne ke liye. Frequently sorted large tables ke liye, SORTED table type pays off karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Redundant SORT wastes CPU — each SORT on 50,000 rows costs O(n log n) operations. Common anti-pattern: SORT inside a LOOP or multiple SORT calls on the same table. Fix: sort once before all READ TABLE calls; or use SORTED table type which maintains order automatically on every APPEND; or use SELECT ... ORDER BY to receive pre-sorted data from the database. For large tables sorted frequently, SORTED table type pays off.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Code review finding: developer sorted lt_materials inside a LOOP at lt_orders (5,000 iterations) — 5,000 redundant sorts of 10,000-row table. Moved SORT before LOOP: from 5,000 × O(n log n) to 1 × O(n log n). Runtime dropped from 8 minutes to 2 seconds for that code block.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Sorting inside a loop because it 'feels safer' to sort before each READ — catastrophic performance. Sort once, read many times.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" BAD: SORT inside LOOP (redundant 5000 times):
LOOP AT lt_orders INTO DATA(ls_o).
  SORT lt_materials BY matnr.  " O(n log n) × 5000 times!
  READ TABLE lt_materials WITH KEY matnr = ls_o-matnr
    BINARY SEARCH INTO DATA(ls_mat).
ENDLOOP.

" GOOD: SORT once before LOOP:
SORT lt_materials BY matnr.    " O(n log n) exactly once
LOOP AT lt_orders INTO DATA(ls_o2).
  READ TABLE lt_materials WITH KEY matnr = ls_o2-matnr
    BINARY SEARCH INTO DATA(ls_mat2).
ENDLOOP.

" BEST: SORTED table type (maintained automatically):
DATA lt_mat_sorted TYPE SORTED TABLE OF ty_mat
  WITH NON-UNIQUE KEY matnr.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the performance impact of deep structures in internal tables?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: STRING, XSTRING, ya nested object references containing deep structures LOOP INTO mein flat structures jo sirf primitive types contain karte hain unke compared mein higher copy costs incur karte hain. STRING field ki har copy mein dynamic memory operations involve hote hain. 100,000+ row loops pe, ye accumulate hota hai. Saara copy overhead eliminate karne ke liye LOOP ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL use karo. Bhi, deep structures pe SORT flat structures se slower hai — ek flat key field pe sort karne aur secondary index approach use karne pe consider karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Deep structures containing STRING, XSTRING, or nested object references incur higher copy costs in LOOP INTO compared to flat structures with only primitive types. Each copy of a STRING field involves dynamic memory operations. On 100,000+ row loops, this accumulates. Use LOOP ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL to eliminate all copy overhead. Also, SORT on deep structures is slower than on flat structures — consider sorting on a flat key field and using a secondary index approach.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Performance investigation: LOOP with 200k rows was slow despite simple logic. Structure had 8 STRING fields (long text descriptions). ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL replacement reduced runtime from 45 seconds to 12 seconds — the 33-second difference was pure structure copy overhead across 200k iterations.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Including large STRING or text fields in internal table structures when only a key field is needed for the LOOP operation — carry unnecessary payload.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Deep structure - STRING fields are expensive to copy:
TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_deep,
         matnr   TYPE matnr,
         maktx   TYPE maktx,    " flat - OK
         longtext TYPE string,  " deep - expensive copy!
         xmldata  TYPE xstring, " deep - very expensive!
       END OF ty_deep.
DATA lt_deep TYPE TABLE OF ty_deep.
" BAD: copies deep fields on every iteration:
LOOP AT lt_deep INTO DATA(ls). ENDLOOP.
" GOOD: no copy, pointer to existing row:
LOOP AT lt_deep ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<r>). ENDLOOP.
" ALSO GOOD: separate flat key table from deep content:
DATA lt_keys TYPE TABLE OF matnr.  " just keys
DATA lt_texts TYPE HASHED TABLE OF ty_deep
  WITH UNIQUE KEY matnr.  " full content, accessed by key

followupAnswerBank.question: How does table type selection impact performance with large data volumes?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Table type selection ek core performance decision hai. Standard table: O(1) APPEND, O(n) READ TABLE (sequential). Sorted table: O(log n) APPEND (order maintain karta hai), O(log n) READ TABLE (automatic binary search). Hashed table: hash ke saath O(1) APPEND, O(1) READ TABLE (hash lookup), undefined LOOP order. Primary operation ke basis pe choose karo: bulk load phir loop = standard; frequent key lookup = sorted ya hashed; fastest lookup ke saath unique key = hashed.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Table type selection is a core performance decision. Standard table: O(1) APPEND, O(n) READ TABLE (sequential). Sorted table: O(log n) APPEND (maintains order), O(log n) READ TABLE (automatic binary search). Hashed table: O(1) APPEND with hash, O(1) READ TABLE (hash lookup), undefined LOOP order. Choose based on primary operation: bulk load then loop = standard; frequent key lookup = sorted or hashed; unique key with fastest lookup = hashed.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Material master lookup: 80,000 materials loaded into standard table. 500,000 READ TABLE calls from a report loop. Each read O(n): 80,000 × 500,000 lookups sequential = disaster. Changed to HASHED table: O(1) per lookup. Report from 4 hours to 8 minutes. Same code, different table type declaration.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using standard table for everything by default — works fine in development with small data, fails catastrophically in production with lookup-heavy patterns.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Lookup-heavy scenario: 500k reads on 80k material rows
" BAD - standard table O(n) each read:
DATA lt_mat_std TYPE TABLE OF ty_mat.  " sequential scan!
READ TABLE lt_mat_std WITH KEY matnr = 'M001' INTO DATA(ls).

" GOOD - hashed table O(1) each read:
DATA lt_mat_hash TYPE HASHED TABLE OF ty_mat
  WITH UNIQUE KEY matnr.
READ TABLE lt_mat_hash WITH TABLE KEY matnr = 'M001' INTO DATA(ls_h).
" 500k reads: standard = 40B operations, hashed = 500k operations
" Performance difference: hours vs seconds

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the impact of INITIAL SIZE on internal table performance?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Table declaration mein INITIAL SIZE specified number of rows ke liye memory pre-allocate karta hai, APPEND ke through table grow hone par repeated memory reallocation avoid karte hue. Hazaron rows hold karne wali expected tables ke liye, INITIAL SIZE specify karna allocation overhead reduce karta hai. Iske bina, ABAP internal thresholds pe memory reallocate karta hai (typically doubling). 100,000 expected rows ke liye, INITIAL SIZE 100000 ya ek reasonable estimate bulk load scenarios mein APPEND performance improve karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: INITIAL SIZE in table declaration pre-allocates memory for the specified number of rows, avoiding repeated memory reallocation as the table grows via APPEND. For tables expected to hold thousands of rows, specifying INITIAL SIZE reduces allocation overhead. Without it, ABAP reallocates memory at internal thresholds (typically doubling). For 100,000 expected rows, INITIAL SIZE 100000 or a reasonable estimate improves APPEND performance in bulk load scenarios.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Bulk data load optimization: loading 500,000 rows via APPEND loop was slow. Adding INITIAL SIZE 500000 to the table declaration reduced table construction time by 30% — purely from eliminating repeated memory reallocation. Small declarative change, meaningful improvement.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Specifying INITIAL SIZE much larger than expected rows — wastes memory upfront. Estimate conservatively: better to slightly underestimate and accept one reallocation than to over-allocate on every program run.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Without INITIAL SIZE: multiple reallocations during APPEND
DATA lt_no_hint TYPE TABLE OF ty_order.
" ABAP reallocates: at 16 rows, 32, 64, 128... up to 500k
" Many realloc operations + data copy at each threshold

" With INITIAL SIZE: one allocation upfront
DATA lt_presized TYPE TABLE OF ty_order
  INITIAL SIZE 500000.
" Single allocation of 500k row capacity
" No reallocation during 500k APPEND loop
" 30% faster table construction for large bulk loads

" Practical usage:
DO 500000 TIMES.
  APPEND VALUE ty_order( vbeln = |{ sy-index }|
    matnr = |M{ sy-index MOD 1000 }| ) TO lt_presized.
ENDDO.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you measure and compare internal table performance before and after a fix?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Performance SAT (ABAP Runtime Analysis) use karke measure karo: production-representative data ke saath fix se pehle SAT trace run karo — total runtime, database call count, memory consumption, aur top 5 statement times record karo. Fix apply karo. Identical data aur parameters ke saath SAT phir run karo. Metrics compare karo: runtime delta, DB calls delta, memory delta. Percentage improvement ke roop mein express karo. Ye objective before/after comparison fix effectiveness demonstrate karne ka professional standard hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Measure performance using SAT (ABAP Runtime Analysis): run SAT trace before fix with production-representative data — record total runtime, database call count, memory consumption, and top 5 statement times. Apply fix. Run SAT again with identical data and parameters. Compare metrics: runtime delta, DB calls delta, memory delta. Express as percentage improvement. This objective before/after comparison is the professional standard for demonstrating fix effectiveness.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Measurement discipline: every performance fix gets a SAT before/after comparison table documented in the ticket. Column headers: metric, before, after, improvement%. Management can understand '4 min → 8 sec (97% improvement)' without any ABAP knowledge. Objective evidence builds trust.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Saying 'I think it is faster now' without measuring — subjective assessment is not credible. Always measure with SAT, always compare objectively.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SAT Before/After Comparison Process:
" BEFORE FIX:
" /nSAT → Activate → Run report with 100k rows → Analyze
" Record:
"   Total Elapsed Time: 4 min 32 sec
"   DB Call Count: 87,643 (SELECT SINGLE in LOOP)
"   Memory Peak: 840 MB
" Apply fix: replace N+1 with pre-fetch FAE
" AFTER FIX:
" /nSAT → Activate → Run SAME report SAME data → Analyze
" Record:
"   Total Elapsed Time: 8 sec  (97% improvement)
"   DB Call Count: 3 (1 main SELECT + 1 FAE + 1 MAKT)
"   Memory Peak: 42 MB (95% reduction)
" Document comparison in ticket for stakeholders

internal-tables-038.easyUnderstanding:
Authorization issue matlab: koi user aisa data access kar raha hai jo usse nahi karna chahiye, ya koi aisa data nahi dekh pa raha jo usse dekhna chahiye — aur iska connection internal table se hai.

Sabse simple example: ek internal table mein saari companies ka data laaya developer. Lekin user sirf Company 1000 dekh sakta hai. Agar internal table mein sari companies ka data hai aur authorization check nahi hua toh user Company 2000 ka data bhi dekh sakta hai ALV report mein. Yeh authorization breach hai.

Doosra example: developer ne SELECT ke andar direct authorization object check nahi lagaya — bina AUTHORITY-CHECK ke data internal table mein aa gaya. ALV mein sab dikh raha hai sab users ko.

Sahih approach: pehle user ki authorization check karo. Phir sirf authorized data SELECT karo. Internal table mein sirf wahi data laao jo user dekhne ka haqdar hai. ALV mein wahi dega.

Aur ek common mistake: internal table mein sab laao, phir DELETE karo unauthorized rows. Yeh galat hai — data pehle toh memory mein aaya. Correct: SELECT mein hi filter karo using authorization fields.

ABAP mein AUTHORITY-CHECK statement authorization object verify karta hai. S_TCODE, S_TABU_DIS, aur custom authorization objects common hain. Internal table building se pehle hamesha authorization check karo.
internal-tables-038.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Internal tables aur authorization ke beech ka relationship aur sahi implementation strategy technically samajhte hain.

Problem 1 — Authorization Bypass via Internal Table:
Developer SELECT FROM vbak karta hai bina company code filter ke. Internal table mein sab companies ka data aa jaata hai. User display mein sab dekh sakta hai — bhaale uska authorization only Company 1000 ke liye ho. Root cause: AUTHORITY-CHECK skip kiya.

Correct Approach — Check Before Load:
STEP 1: AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'V_VBAK_BUK' ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_bukrs ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'. IF sy-subrc <> 0. MESSAGE 'No authorization' TYPE 'E'. RETURN. ENDIF. STEP 2: SELECT FROM vbak WHERE bukrs = lv_bukrs. Ab internal table mein sirf authorized company ka data.

Problem 2 — Multi-Company Scenario:
User multiple companies ke liye authorized hai — 1000, 2000. Pehle user ke authorized companies ki list nikalo (SUSR_USER_AUTH_FOR_OBJ_GET ya authority range loop). Phir SELECT mein IN list use karo sirf authorized companies ke liye. Internal table mein sirf authorized data.

Problem 3 — Data Level vs Transaction Level Authorization:
Transaction ke liye authorization alag hai. Data ke liye authorization alag hai. User SE38 run kar sakta hai (transaction authorized) lekin specific table data nahi dekh sakta (data authorization). Internal table ka data always data-level authorization se bound hona chahiye.

Problem 4 — Sensitive Fields in Internal Table:
Internal table mein sensitive fields hain — salary (HR), bank account (FI). Agar ALV mein dikh rahe hain toh authorization required hai. Solution: agar user sensitive field nahi dekh sakta toh either field hi internal table structure mein mat laao, ya display layer mein column hide karo based on AUTHORITY-CHECK result.

Problem 5 — Authorization in Background Jobs:
Background jobs mein user context alag hota hai — batch user. Internal table pe data load hota hai batch user ke authorization ke hisaab se, nahi ki requesting user ke hisaab se. Scheduling permissions aur batch user authorization separately configure karo.

Problem 6 — ABAP Test Cockpit Authorization Checks:
ATC checks hain jo verify karte hain AUTHORITY-CHECK correctly use ho raha hai — 'Authorization check missing' warnings ATC raise karta hai for programs accessing sensitive tables. Production mein transport blocker ban sakta hai.

Best Practice Summary:
1. AUTHORITY-CHECK before any data operation, not after.
2. SELECT filter should use authorized fields — never load all then delete.
3. Display layer authorization for sensitive columns separately.
4. Background job batch user authorization carefully managed.
5. ATC checks pass karna mandatory for governance compliance.
internal-tables-038.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'Authorization issues with internal tables typically arise when data is loaded without proper authorization checks — the internal table ends up containing data the user should not see, and it gets displayed in ALV or passed to other components.'

'The correct approach is: perform AUTHORITY-CHECK before the SELECT statement, restrict the SELECT to only authorized data using WHERE clause on authorization-relevant fields like BUKRS or WERKS, and never load all data into internal table and then filter it — data should not enter the internal table if unauthorized.'

'A second common scenario is sensitive fields — like salary or bank data. If these fields are in the internal table structure, I ensure AUTHORITY-CHECK is done before loading them and display is conditional on authorization result. Sometimes the cleanest approach is to exclude sensitive fields from the structure entirely if the user is not authorized.'

Hinglish tip: Authorization question pe always 2 parts bolna — transaction-level vs data-level authorization. Aur yeh bolna ki 'load first delete later' approach WRONG hai — data pehle memory mein aata hai jo security risk hai. Sahi approach: SELECT mein hi filter karo. Yeh security-aware thinking dikhata hai.
internal-tables-038.codeExamples:
" === Correct Authorization Check Before Internal Table Load ===

" Step 1: Check authorization for company code
DATA lv_bukrs TYPE bukrs VALUE '1000'.
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_BKPF_BUK'
  ID 'BUKRS'  FIELD lv_bukrs
  ID 'ACTVT'  FIELD '03'.  " 03 = Display
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'No authorization for company 1000' TYPE 'E'.
  RETURN.
ENDIF.

" Step 2: Load ONLY authorized data into internal table
SELECT belnr, bukrs, bldat, dmbtr
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_invoices)
  FROM bkpf
  WHERE bukrs = @lv_bukrs  " already auth-checked company
    AND gjahr = @sy-datum(4).

" Step 3: Multi-company - collect authorized companies first
DATA lt_auth_bukrs TYPE TABLE OF bukrs.
DATA lv_all_bukrs TYPE bukrs.
DO.
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_BKPF_BUK'
    ID 'BUKRS'  FIELD lv_all_bukrs
    ID 'ACTVT'  FIELD '03'.
  IF sy-subrc = 0. APPEND lv_all_bukrs TO lt_auth_bukrs. ENDIF.
  " Iterate through company codes...
  EXIT.  " simplified for example
ENDDO.
IF lt_auth_bukrs IS NOT INITIAL.
  SELECT belnr, bukrs, bldat, dmbtr
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_all_inv)
    FROM bkpf
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @lt_auth_bukrs
    WHERE bukrs = @lt_auth_bukrs-table_line.
ENDIF.
internal-tables-038.interviewScriptHinglish: authorization issues internal tables ke saath tab arise hote hain jab data bina proper checks ke load hota hai aur table mein woh rows hoti hain jo user ko nahi dikhni chahiye, jo phir ALV outputs ya downstream services mein leak hoti hain. Correct approach hai SELECT se pehle AUTHORITY-CHECK perform karna, BUKRS ya WERKS jaise authorization-relevant fields pe WHERE clauses se SELECT restrict karna, aur dangerous load-everything-then-delete-unauthorized pattern avoid karna. Multi-company reports ke liye main pehle authorised company codes ka driver build karta hoon aur FOR ALL ENTRIES sirf un pe run karta hoon. Sensitive fields jaise salary ya bank data AUTHORITY-CHECK success pe conditionally load hote hain aur ALV output mein hide ho jaate hain jab allowed nahi hote.
internal-tables-038.interviewScriptEnglish: Authorization issues with internal tables arise when data is loaded without proper checks and the table contains rows the user should not see, which then leak into ALV outputs or downstream services. The correct approach is to perform AUTHORITY-CHECK before the SELECT, restrict the SELECT using WHERE clauses on authorization-relevant fields like BUKRS or WERKS, and avoid the dangerous load-everything-then-delete-unauthorized pattern. For multi-company reports I build a driver of authorised company codes first and run FOR ALL ENTRIES only over them. Sensitive fields like salary or bank data are loaded conditionally on AUTHORITY-CHECK success and hidden from ALV output when not allowed.

wordByWordSamjho.meaning: Checks display authorisation for accounting documents at company code level.
Restricts the authorisation check to a specific company code.
Activity 03 represents Display access in standard SAP.

wordByWordSamjho.keyword: SELECT belnr, bukrs, bldat, dmbtr FROM bkpf
wordByWordSamjho.meaning: Loads accounting headers using only the fields needed for the report.
Restricts the result to the authorisation-checked company code.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Loading all data and deleting unauthorised rows from the internal table afterwards.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Sensitive data still passes through memory and can leak via logs, debugger or downstream APIs — a serious compliance risk.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Always perform AUTHORITY-CHECK first and filter the SELECT with WHERE on authorization-relevant fields so unauthorised rows never enter memory.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Reusing one AUTHORITY-CHECK result for many company codes.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Authorisation may be granted for some company codes only; using one check masks failures for others.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Loop over relevant company codes, call AUTHORITY-CHECK per BUKRS, build a driver of authorised codes and SELECT via FOR ALL ENTRIES.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Skipping AUTHORITY-CHECK in background jobs assuming the system handles it.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Background jobs inherit RFC user authorisations; missing checks expose sensitive data to unintended users via spool or output files.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Apply the same AUTHORITY-CHECK plus WHERE filtering in background reports and verify with SUIM trace.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Displaying sensitive fields in ALV without column-level authorization.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Even if rows are filtered, sensitive fields like salary or bank account leak when the user lacks field level access.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Hide or mask sensitive fields in ALV based on AUTHORITY-CHECK, or load them conditionally and skip them in the field catalogue.

internal-tables-038.quickRevisionNotes:
What: Authorization patterns for safe internal table data loading and display.

Used for:
- Multi company financial and sales reports
- Background jobs and interfaces that bypass dialog auths
- Compliance-driven access on sensitive HR or finance data

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_BKPF_BUK' before SELECT
- WHERE bukrs IN @lt_auth_bukrs; FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @lt_auth_bukrs
- Verify via SU53 failed checks; SUIM authorisation trace

Interview keyword: AUTHORITY-CHECK before SELECT, filter in WHERE, never load-then-delete.

followupAnswerBank.question: What ABAP authorization objects are most commonly used with internal table data loading?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Internal table data loading ke liye commonly used authorization objects: company code ke hisab se filter kiye financial documents ke liye F_BKPF_BUK; company ke hisab se sales orders ke liye V_VBAK_BUK; plant ke hisab se material movements ke liye M_MSEG_WMB; HR master data ke liye P_ORGIN; general table content display ke liye S_TABU_DIS. Hamesha loaded ho rahe data ke liye relevant authorization object identify karo aur SELECT statement se pehle AUTHORITY-CHECK ke saath check karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Commonly used authorization objects for internal table data loading: F_BKPF_BUK for financial documents filtered by company code; V_VBAK_BUK for sales orders by company; M_MSEG_WMB for material movements by plant; P_ORGIN for HR master data; S_TABU_DIS for general table content display. Always identify the relevant authorization object for the data being loaded and check it with AUTHORITY-CHECK before the SELECT statement.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Custom report development: FI document report needed authorization for company code (F_BKPF_BUK) and document type (F_BKPF_BLA). Both checks done before SELECT. QA found one check was missing in initial build — fixed before transport. Authorization gaps found in development cost nothing; gaps found in production cost reputation.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using only S_TCODE authorization (transaction access) and assuming that is sufficient — transaction access is separate from data access. Always check data-level authorization objects too.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" FI: Check before loading financial documents
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_BKPF_BUK'
  ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_bukrs ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
CHECK sy-subrc = 0.
" Sales: Check before loading sales orders
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'V_VBAK_BUK'
  ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_bukrs ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
CHECK sy-subrc = 0.
" Materials: Check before loading movements
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'M_MSEG_WMB'
  ID 'WERKS' FIELD lv_werks ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
CHECK sy-subrc = 0.
" Then load internal table only after all checks pass

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle authorization for internal tables in multi-company-code reports?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Multi-company reports ke liye: pehle user ke authorized company codes ko relevant companies ke through loop karke aur har ek ke liye AUTHORITY-CHECK karke collect karo, ya SUSR_USER_AUTH_FOR_OBJ_GET function module use karo. Authorized companies ko ek internal table mein store karo. Phir authorized companies table ke saath SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES use karo — internal table sirf authorized data receive karta hai. Saari companies load karke unauthorized ones delete kabhi mat karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: For multi-company reports: first collect the user's authorized company codes by looping through relevant companies and checking AUTHORITY-CHECK for each, or use SUSR_USER_AUTH_FOR_OBJ_GET function module. Store authorized companies in an internal table. Then use SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES with the authorized companies table — internal table receives only authorized data. Never load all companies and delete unauthorized ones.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Cross-company financial report: used SUSR_USER_AUTH_FOR_OBJ_GET to get user's authorized company codes for F_BKPF_BUK. Returned list used as FAE driving table for SELECT FROM bkpf. User only sees their authorized companies automatically — no hardcoded company list needed. Solution scales as user authorization changes without code modification.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Hardcoding company code lists in the SELECT — when user authorization changes or new companies are added, the code breaks. Authorization should be dynamic based on user's actual authorization profile.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Dynamic multi-company authorization:
DATA lt_auth_bukrs TYPE TABLE OF bukrs.
" Option 1: manual loop for known companies
DATA lt_all_companies TYPE TABLE OF bukrs.
" (populate lt_all_companies from T001 or selection screen)
LOOP AT lt_all_companies INTO DATA(lv_co).
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_BKPF_BUK'
    ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_co ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    APPEND lv_co TO lt_auth_bukrs.
  ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.
" Load only authorized companies:
IF lt_auth_bukrs IS NOT INITIAL.
  SELECT belnr, bukrs, bldat, dmbtr
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_docs)
    FROM bkpf
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @lt_auth_bukrs
    WHERE bukrs = @lt_auth_bukrs-table_line.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the security risk of loading all data into an internal table and then deleting unauthorized rows?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Saara data load karke phir unauthorized rows delete karne ki do problems hain: pehle, unauthorized data thodi der ke liye program memory mein exist karta hai — chahe ABAP ke isolated work processes mein low risk hai, ye minimal data exposure ke principle ko violate karta hai; doosre, ye needed se zyada data fetch karke resources waste karta hai. Correct approach hai WHERE clause mein authorization-checked field values use karke SELECT level pe filter karna — unauthorized data kabhi system mein enter nahi karta.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Loading all data then deleting unauthorized rows has two problems: first, unauthorized data briefly exists in program memory — while this is low risk in ABAP's isolated work processes, it violates the principle of minimal data exposure; second, it wastes resources by fetching more data than needed. The correct approach is to filter at the SELECT level using authorization-checked field values in the WHERE clause — unauthorized data never enters the system.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Security audit finding: custom report loaded all HR salary data into internal table then deleted rows where user lacked authorization. Auditor flagged this — 'salary data should never be in memory without authorization'. Refactored to AUTHORITY-CHECK before SELECT with personnel area filter. Passed re-audit.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Thinking DELETE from internal table after loading is equivalent to never loading — from a security and audit perspective, it is not. Data existed in memory, even briefly.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" WRONG (security anti-pattern):
SELECT pernr, gross_pay, bukrs INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_all_hr)
  FROM pa0008.                          " ALL salary data in memory!
DELETE lt_all_hr WHERE bukrs <> lv_user_bukrs.  " delete after
" Unauthorized salary data was in lt_all_hr momentarily

" CORRECT (filter at source):
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'P_ORGIN'
  ID 'PERSA' FIELD lv_persa ID 'INFTY' FIELD '0008'
  ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
IF sy-subrc <> 0. RETURN. ENDIF.
SELECT pernr, gross_pay, bukrs
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_hr_auth)
  FROM pa0008
  WHERE persa = @lv_persa.  " only authorized data loaded

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you implement column-level authorization for sensitive fields in ALV reports?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: ALV mein column-level authorization: sensitive field ke authorization object ke liye AUTHORITY-CHECK perform karo. Agar authorized — column visible rakho. Agar nahi — ya toh field ko internal table structure se entirely exclude karo (cleanest, memory mein koi data nahi), ya include karo lekin column->SET_VISIBLE( abap_false ) use karke ALV column hide karo. Cleanest security approach hai ki agar user authorized nahi hai toh sensitive data load mat karo — structure mein koi field nahi, memory mein koi data nahi, hide karne ke liye koi column nahi.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Column-level authorization in ALV: perform AUTHORITY-CHECK for the sensitive field's authorization object. If authorized — keep the column visible. If not — either exclude the field from the internal table structure entirely (cleanest, no data in memory), or include it but hide the ALV column using column->SET_VISIBLE( abap_false ). The cleanest security approach is to not load sensitive data if the user is not authorized — no field in structure, no data in memory, no column to hide.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: HR report with optional salary column: AUTHORITY-CHECK on P_HAP_DOC for compensation data. Authorized users see salary column populated and visible. Non-authorized users — salary field excluded from SELECT, column not added to ALV. Data-level and display-level both secured. Audit passed cleanly.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Loading sensitive field into internal table but only hiding the ALV column — data is still in memory and could be exposed via debugger or other means. True security requires not loading the data at all.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Column-level authorization for salary field:
DATA lv_salary_auth TYPE abap_bool VALUE abap_false.
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'P_ORGIN'
  ID 'PERSA' FIELD lv_persa
  ID 'INFTY' FIELD '0008'  " Infotype 8 = Basic Pay
  ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
IF sy-subrc = 0.
  lv_salary_auth = abap_true.
ENDIF.
" Load structure based on authorization:
IF lv_salary_auth = abap_true.
  SELECT pernr, ename, lgart, betrg  " include salary
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_with_pay) FROM pa0008
    WHERE persa = @lv_persa.
ELSE.
  SELECT pernr, ename              " exclude salary field
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_no_pay) FROM pa0001
    WHERE persa = @lv_persa.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do authorization issues appear differently in foreground vs background execution?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Foreground execution mein, AUTHORITY-CHECK dialog user ke authorization profile ke against validate karta hai — failures error messages dikhati hain. Background (batch) execution mein, authorization SM36/SM37 job definition mein specified batch user ke against check hoti hai. Ek common issue: report dialog user ke liye fine kaam karti hai lekin background mein fail hoti hai kyunki batch user mein required authorization objects nahi hain. Fix: SU01 ke through batch user ko necessary authorization objects assign karo. SAP_ALL wale batch user kabhi use mat karo — minimal required authorizations assign karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: In foreground execution, AUTHORITY-CHECK validates against the dialog user's authorization profile — failures show error messages. In background (batch) execution, authorization is checked against the batch user specified in SM36/SM37 job definition. A common issue: report works fine for dialog user but fails in background because the batch user lacks required authorization objects. Fix: assign necessary authorization objects to the batch user via SU01. Never use a batch user with SAP_ALL — assign minimal required authorizations.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Production issue: nightly financial report ran fine for users but batch job failed with authorization error. SM37 showed dump — batch user BATCH_FI lacked F_BKPF_BUK authorization for company 2000 which was newly added. Basis team added authorization to batch user. Job ran successfully next night. Root cause: new company added, batch user profile not updated.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Assigning SAP_ALL to batch users for convenience — this is a major security risk and audit failure. Always assign minimal required authorizations to batch users.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Authorization-aware background execution:
" Check if running in background (sy-batch = 'X')
IF sy-batch = 'X'.
  " Running as batch job - batch user's auth checked
  " Log authorization context for audit trail
  MESSAGE |Background run: user={ sy-uname }| TYPE 'I'
    INTO DATA(lv_log).
ENDIF.
" AUTHORITY-CHECK works same way in foreground/background
" but checks different user's profile
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_BKPF_BUK'
  ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_bukrs
  ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  " In background: dump or write to application log
  " Not MESSAGE 'E' which doesn't work in background
  RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_no_authority.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you audit-trail internal table data access for compliance?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Internal table data access pe compliance audit trails ke liye: Business Application Log (BAL) use karo — jab bhi sensitive data load ho log entries create karne ke liye SBAL function modules call karo. Record karo: user (sy-uname), timestamp (sy-datum + sy-uzeit), accessed data scope (company code, date range, material range), aur authorization check result. Logs SLG1 transaction mein viewable hain. Long-term retention ke liye, appropriate archiving ke saath ek custom Z audit table mein likho.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: For compliance audit trails on internal table data access: use Business Application Log (BAL) — call SBAL function modules to create log entries whenever sensitive data is loaded. Record: user (sy-uname), timestamp (sy-datum + sy-uzeit), data scope accessed (company code, date range, material range), and authorization check result. Logs viewable in SLG1 transaction. For long-term retention, write to a custom Z audit table with appropriate archiving.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: SOX compliance project: all financial document reads had to be audit-logged. Added BAL logging call after every SELECT FROM bkpf — log entry: user, company code, date range, row count fetched. SLG1 report made available to internal auditors. Compliance team satisfied. Minimal performance impact — BAL call after SELECT, not inside loop.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Logging inside the LOOP (one log entry per row) instead of after the SELECT (one entry summarizing the access) — creates millions of log entries and massive performance overhead.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Audit logging for internal table data access:
DATA lv_log_handle TYPE balloghndl.
" Open log
CALL FUNCTION 'BAL_LOG_CREATE'
  EXPORTING
    i_s_log = VALUE bal_s_log(
      extnumber = |FI_DOC_ACCESS_{ sy-datum }|
      object    = 'ZFIN_AUDIT' )
  IMPORTING e_log_handle = lv_log_handle.
" Load internal table (authorized)
SELECT belnr, bukrs, dmbtr INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_docs)
  FROM bkpf WHERE bukrs = @lv_bukrs AND gjahr = @lv_gjahr.
" Log the access AFTER load (not inside loop)
CALL FUNCTION 'BAL_LOG_MSG_ADD'
  EXPORTING i_log_handle = lv_log_handle
    i_s_msg = VALUE bal_s_msg(
      msgty = 'I' msgid = 'ZFIN'
      msgno = '001'
      msgv1 = sy-uname msgv2 = lv_bukrs
      msgv3 = lv_gjahr msgv4 = lines( lt_docs ) ).

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle authorization errors gracefully in internal table-driven reports?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Graceful authorization error handling: ek meaningful user-friendly message dikhao explain karte hue ki kya restricted hai aur kyun (sirf 'No authorization' nahi). Partial authorization ke liye, sirf authorized data load aur display karo aur user ko clearly indicate karo ki kuch data restricted hai. Technical error messages avoid karo — 'Authorization object V_VBAK_BUK check failed' end users ke liye kuch nahi matlab. Security monitoring ke liye authorization failure log karo. Reports ke liye, ALV header mein 'Data restricted to companies 1000, 2000 per your authorization' dikhane wala ek status message professional hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Graceful authorization error handling: show a meaningful user-friendly message explaining what is restricted and why (not just 'No authorization'). For partial authorization, load and display only authorized data and clearly indicate to the user that some data is restricted. Avoid technical error messages — 'Authorization object V_VBAK_BUK check failed' means nothing to end users. Log the authorization failure for security monitoring. For reports, a status message in the ALV header showing 'Data restricted to companies 1000, 2000 per your authorization' is professional.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: User-friendly auth handling: instead of dumping or showing cryptic message, report showed 'Displaying data for companies 1000, 2000 based on your authorization. Company 3000 data is not accessible with your current role. Contact BASIS for access.' Users appreciated the clarity. Support tickets for auth issues dropped significantly.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Showing technical authorization error messages to end users — confusing and unhelpful. Always translate authorization failures into business-language messages.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Graceful authorization handling:
DATA lt_authorized_companies TYPE TABLE OF bukrs.
DATA lt_restricted_companies  TYPE TABLE OF bukrs.
LOOP AT lt_requested_companies INTO DATA(lv_bukrs).
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'V_VBAK_BUK'
    ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_bukrs ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    APPEND lv_bukrs TO lt_authorized_companies.
  ELSE.
    APPEND lv_bukrs TO lt_restricted_companies.  " note restricted
  ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.
" Load only authorized data:
IF lt_authorized_companies IS NOT INITIAL.
  SELECT ... FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @lt_authorized_companies ...
ENDIF.
" Inform user about restrictions:
IF lt_restricted_companies IS NOT INITIAL.
  DATA(lv_msg) = |Data restricted for: |
    && CONCAT_LINES_OF( table = lt_restricted_companies sep = ', ' ).
  MESSAGE lv_msg TYPE 'W'.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does internal table authorization differ from database table authorization in SAP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Database table authorization (S_TABU_DIS, S_TABU_NAM) SE16, SM30, aur similar tools ke through direct table access control karti hai. Custom programs mein internal table authorization separate hai — developer ko ABAP program mein explicitly AUTHORITY-CHECK statements code karne padte hain. SE16 access ko ek table tak restrict karna automatically ek custom program ko us table ka data load karne se restrict nahi karta. Har custom program ko apne authorization checks chahiye us data ke liye explicitly coded jo ye internal tables mein load karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Database table authorization (S_TABU_DIS, S_TABU_NAM) controls direct table access via SE16, SM30, and similar tools. Internal table authorization in custom programs is separate — the developer must explicitly code AUTHORITY-CHECK statements in the ABAP program. Restricting SE16 access to a table does not automatically restrict a custom program from loading that table's data. Each custom program needs its own authorization checks coded explicitly for the data it loads into internal tables.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Security gap found in audit: SE16 access to PA0008 (salary) was restricted via S_TABU_DIS for most users. But a custom HR report loaded PA0008 without AUTHORITY-CHECK — all users running the custom report saw salary data. Fix: added P_ORGIN authorization check before SELECT in the custom report. Both layers now secured independently.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Assuming S_TABU_DIS restriction on a table automatically prevents custom programs from reading that table — it does not. Custom programs need explicit AUTHORITY-CHECK in their code.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" S_TABU_DIS controls SE16/SM30 - NOT custom programs!
" SE16 access to PA0008 restricted to BASIS only
" But this custom program has NO automatic restriction:
SELECT pernr, lgart, betrg INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_salary)
  FROM pa0008.  " Anyone running this program sees salary!
" WRONG: relying on S_TABU_DIS for custom program security

" CORRECT: explicit AUTHORITY-CHECK in custom program:
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'P_ORGIN'
  ID 'PERSA' FIELD lv_persa
  ID 'INFTY' FIELD '0008'
  ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'Salary data not authorized' TYPE 'E'. RETURN.
ENDIF.
SELECT pernr, lgart, betrg INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_salary_auth)
  FROM pa0008 WHERE persa = @lv_persa.
